cases.case:
A 55-year-old male presents to the emergency room with chest pain which started after
mowing the lawn. EKG is below.
interpretation: PR interval (normal or abnormal): abnormal (no P waves)

ST segment-T wave changes: ST elevations in leads II, III, aVF, V3, V4. Reciprocal
depressions in lead aVL, V1, V2
Interpretation: inferior STEMI with atrial fibrillation
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows an inferior STEMI with ST elevations in leads II, III, and aVF. There
is likely occlusion of the right coronary artery. Given there are also ST elevations in V3 and V4, it
is possible that there are multiple coronary arteries with chronic atherosclerotic disease in
addition to the acutely occluded RCA. There is additionally an irregularly irregular rhythm
without P waves, consistent with atrial fibrillation.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
This patient is having a STEMI, so the cardiac catheterization lab
should be activated and the patient should be taken urgently for revascularization. Additional
therapies include aspirin, heparin/antiplatelet agents, and a beta blocker if there are no signs of
shock or heart failure.
cases.clinical_pearls:
Inferior MI caused by proximal right coronary artery can result in RV infarction.
These patients are preload dependent and as such can become very hypotensive with nitrates
which reduce preload. In these cases, you may need to give fluid to support cardiac output.

cases.case:
A 35-year-old female comes to the clinic with several weeks of fatigue and dry skin. An EKG
is done which is shown below.
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows sinus bradycardia with a rate of 42 and normal PR interval so
no evidence of AV block.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
This patient has symptoms concerning for hypothyroidism,
so we should check thyroid function tests (TSH & T4).
cases.clinical_pearl:
There are many causes of sinus bradycardia. Nonpharmacological causes
include hypothyroidism, sinus node disease, inferior MI (where the sinus node is infarcted),
increased vagal tone (athletes), and hypothermia. Pharmacological causes include beta
blockers, calcium channel blockers, amiodarone, opioids and GABA-ergic medications
(barbiturates, benzodiazepines).

cases.case:
A 66-year-old male with a history of hypertension presents to the emergency room
with palpitations. EKG is below.
basic_interpretation: PR interval: abnormal – 2nd degree AV block Mobitz 1
ST-T changes: upsloping ST elevations V3-V4, T wave inversions I and aVL
Other findings: poor R wave progression
Interpretation: 2nd degree AV block Mobitz 1/Wenckebach; LBBB
cases.explanation:
There are several abnormalities in this EKG, which are more easily identified if
a systematic approach to review is employed.
First, you’ll notice that the ventricular rate is slow – this is because although the sinus node
is firing appropriately (as evidenced by our normal atrial rate of 66), there is a 2nd degree AV
block which is preventing some of the impulses spreading to the ventricles. So the rhythm
is still sinus, but the AV block is preventing all of the sinus beats to conduct appropriately.
Continuing with our systematic read, we see that the QRS complex is positive in lead I but
negative in lead II and aVF, which is consistent with a left axis deviation.
Then we come to our PR interval – looking at our rhythm strip the first PR interval we see is
long (approx 400 ms or 2 large boxes). The next P wave is not followed by a QRS complex.
The next PR interval is also prolonged, approximately 400 ms. The following PR interval is
even longer, approximately 480 ms. The next P wave is again not followed by a QRS
complex. So we have a prolonged PR interval, dropped QRS complexes, and a progressive
lengthening of the PR interval prior to a dropped beat, making this a second degree AV
block Mobitz type 1, or Wenckebach.
The QRS interval is also abnormal. It is prolonged with QRS slightly longer than 120 ms.
There is a prominent S wave in lead V1, making this a left bundle branch block.
There are also some ST wave changes which are likely related to the LBBB – there an
upsloping ST elevations in leads V2-V4 and T wave inversions in leads I and aVL.
Another finding here is poor R wave progression. In a normal EKG, there is a dominant S
wave in lead V1, and then a gradually increasing R wave throughout the precordial leads
until there is a dominant R wave by lead V4. This finding is nonspecific but is associated
with prior anteroseptal MI, LVH or RVH.
resources_further_reading:
Burns, Ed, Robert Buttner, and Ed Burns and Robert Buttner. “AV Block: 2nd Degree,
Mobitz I (Wenckebach Phenomenon).” Life in the Fast Lane • LITFL (blog), August 1,
2020. https://litfl.com/av-block-2nd-degree-mobitz-i-wenckebach-phenomenon/.

“Poor R Wave Progression (PRWP).” Life in the Fast Lane • LITFL (blog), August
1, 2018. https://litfl.com/poor-r-wave-progression-prwp-ecg-library/.

Buttner, Robert, Ed Burns, and Robert Buttner and Ed Burns. “Left Bundle Branch
Block (LBBB).” Life in the Fast Lane • LITFL (blog), January 3, 2021.
https://litfl.com/left-bundle-branch-block-lbbb-ecg-library/.

cases.case:
A 66-year-old male with a PMH of HTN and coronary artery disease who is admitted to the
hospital for pneumonia complains of acute onset of palpitations. Telemetry showed a
sudden increase in heart rates over 150. 12 lead EKG is done and is shown below.
basic_interpretation: ST-T changes: T wave inversions in lead I, II, aVL, V4-V6
Interpretation: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows tachycardia and an irregularly irregular rhythm, with absent P
waves. This is atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
The next best step in management depends on the
patient’s stability. If his blood pressure was low or he had any signs of end-organ
dysfunction like altered mental status, we would consider this unstable atrial fibrillation.
The next step would be synchronized cardioversion.
If the blood pressure is normal and there are no signs of end-organ dysfunction, we can try
to lower the rate with IV metoprolol or diltiazem. You could then try pharmacologic
cardioversion with the initiation of amiodarone.
cases.clinical_pearls:
For new-onset atrial fibrillation (a reliable duration <48 hours), we can
assume that there has not been enough time for the development of a left atrial appendage
clot. In this case, we can safely use chemical (i.e. amiodarone) or electrical cardioversion
without prior anticoagulation. In a patient with a longer duration of atrial fibrillation or
unknown duration (i.e. they present to the ED after several days or weeks of symptoms),
we should get a TEE to ensure there is no left atrial appendage clot prior to attempting
cardioversion. However, if the patient is clinically unstable (low blood pressure, end organ
dysfunction) due to their rapid rates, we still need to proceed with synchronized
cardioversion.
The need for anticoagulation in atrial fibrillation is generally based on the patient’s
CHADS2VASC score, which estimates the annual stroke risk. For this patient with age 66
and history of HTN and vascular disease (CAD), his score is at least 3 and he would be a
candidate for anticoagulation.

cases.case:
A 59-year-old male presents to the emergency room with several hours of crushing
chest pain. He loses consciousness and becomes unresponsive. EKG is below.
basic_interpretation: Rhythm: wide complex tachycardia: ventricular tachycardia
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows a wide complex tachycardia. There are few causes of this and the most
important to consider (and which is the case here) is ventricular tachycardia. The rhythm is
fairly regular with wide QRS complexes. The QRS complexes appear similar in morphology
to each other, making this monomorphic ventricular tachycardia.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
You should follow ACLS guidelines here. Since this patient
is unstable (he has lost consciousness), you should perform synchronized electrical
cardioversion.
cases.clinical_pearl:
Ischemic heart disease is an important cause of ventricular arrhythmias.
After cardioversion, this patient should have a repeat EKG to evaluate for ischemia.

cases.case: A 36-year-old male comes to the emergency room with chest pain. EKG is below.
basic_interpretation: ST segment – T wave changes: none
cases.explanation:
This is an example of a normal EKG. The rhythm is sinus with a normal rate. All of the
intervals are normal and there are no additional abnormal findings.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
You should obtain more history from the patient about the nature of
his chest pain (i.e. brought on with exertion/relieved by rest, location of the pain). If this is
concerning for cardiac chest pain (substernal, brought on by exertion and relieved by rest),
you should obtain a troponin and consider further workup such as stress testing. If it sounds atypical,
consider other causes of chest pain and obtain appropriate studies such as a chest x-ray.

cases.case:
A 75-year-old female presents to the emergency department after several episodes of
syncope. EKG is below.
interpretation: Rhythm: underlying rhythm is sinus but w/complete AV dissociation
QRS interval (normal or abnormal): can’t tell for sure but looks like a RBB
ST segment-T wave changes: isolated ST depression in V4

Interpretation: complete heart block / 3rd degree AV block with slow ventricular escape
rhythm
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows complete heart block or third-degree AV block. We can see that
the P waves occur at regular intervals, but do not have any relationship to the QRS complexes,
which also march out at their own rate. There is a ventricular escape rhythm as the impulses
from the SA node are not being conducted through the AV node.
The most important causes of CHB include advanced age, inferior myocardial infarction, AV
nodal blocking drugs (beta blockers, calcium channel blockers), and degeneration of the
conduction system.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
This patient should be admitted to the hospital for telemetry
monitoring. If their blood pressure is stable, they can be monitored with cutaneous pacer pads
on until permanent pacemaker insertion. If they are unstable, they should receive atropine,
have a temporary pacing wire placed and be monitored in an ICU setting until permanent
pacemaker insertion.
cases.clinical_pearls:
Recall that there are potential pacemakers throughout all chambers of the heart,
and there is an intrinsic rate in each area – 60-100 in the SA node, ~60 in the atria, 40-60 in the
AV node, and 20-40 in the ventricles. Because there is a complete block here at the level of the
AV node, the ventricles are the only remaining pacemaker and so the ventricular rate is called a
ventricular escape rhythm. Because the ventricles do not conduct down the His-Purkinje
system, the impulse must spread cell to cell, which can result in a left or right bundle
morphology.

cases.case:
A 17 year old female with a history of an eating disorder presents to the emergency room for
lethargy. EKG is below.
interpretation: PR interval (normal or abnormal): approximately normal, maybe slightly prolonged
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows profound sinus bradycardia. There is additionally the presence of U waves, best
seen in lead V3. U waves are a common finding in sinus bradycardia.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
Assess for clinical stability (blood pressure). If unstable, administer atropine. If stable, assess for
electrolyte abnormalities.
cases.clinical_pearls:
Anorexia nervosa commonly causes sinus bradycardia, due to decreased metabolic rate,
increased parasympathetic activity, and malnutrition which can result in structural cardiac
abnormalities. Other important causes of sinus bradycardia include ischemia (inferior MI),
hypoxia, hypoglycemia, hypothyroidism, and medications (BBs/CCBs).

cases.case: A 45 year old male presents to the emergency room with complaint of palpitations.
basic_interpretation: PR interval: unable to say exactly, but there is a two to one block
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows a narrow QRS-complex tachycardia with a regular rate and
saw tooth baseline, consistent with atrial flutter. The atrial flutter rhythm originates from a
wave traveling around the atrium and characteristically the arrhythmia circuit includes the
tricuspid isthmus; the atrial rate around 280-300 bpm. It is rare for this rate to be
conducted 1:1 to the ventricles, and if it is, it is very unstable and typically degenerates to
ventricular fibrillation. The most common AV block that is present with atrial flutter is 2:1,
meaning that the AV node conducts one impulse to the ventricles for every 2 flutter atrial
beats. This results in a ventricular rate around 140-150. Higher degrees of AV block can be
present, and these are usually associated with underlying AV nodal disease or medications
such as beta blockers.
Just by looking at this EKG, it seems that the patient may have LVH based on how tall those
QRS complexes are. However, they do not quite meet voltage criteria for LVH, which
include:
- R wave in lead I + S wave in lead II > 25 mm
- R wave in aVL > 11 mm
- R wave in aVF > 20 mm
- S wave in aVR > 14 mm
- R wave in V4, V5, or V6 > 26 mm
- R wave in V5 or V6 plus S wave in V1 > 35 mm
- Largest R wave plus largest S wave in precordial leads > 45 mm
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
The NBSIM here all depends on patient stability. If the
blood pressure is low, this patient requires urgent electrical cardioversion. However, if the
patient is stable, we have some more time to manage this. Atrial flutter, like atrial
fibrillation, has an increased risk of left atrial thrombus. If we don’t know the duration of
atrial flutter, the patient should have a TEE to assess for LA thrombus prior to electrical
cardioversion. They should also be started on anticoagulation based on their
CHADS2VASC score. While rate control medications can be attempted, they have lower
success in atrial flutter versus atrial fibrillation. Ablation of the cavo-tricuspid isthmus can
stop and prevent reoccurrence of atrial flutter.
cases.clinical_pearls:
Atrial flutter is commonly associated with underlying heart or lung disease.
In a patient presenting with a first episode of atrial flutter without known history of cardiac
or pulmonary conditions, you should evaluate for pulmonary conditions (PE, pneumonia,
COPD), underlying structural heart disease (obtain an echocardiogram), and check a TSH
to evaluate for hyperthyroidism.

cases.case:
A 60-year-old male with a history of HTN and osteoarthritis presents for pre-operative
evaluation prior to knee replacement surgery. EKG is below.
basic_interpretation: ST-T changes: ST depressions and T wave inversions in lead II, III, aVF, V4-V6
cases.explanation:
This EKG demonstrates sinus rhythm with left ventricular hypertrophy. The magnitude of
the QRS complexes in this EKG jumps out as being abnormal, but there are specific criteria
for identifying LVH on EKG. There are both voltage and non-voltage criteria, both of which
have to be met. The most used voltage criteria are the Sokolow-Lyon criteria: R wave in V5
or V6 plus S wave in V1 > 35 mm. There are several others:
- R wave in lead I + S wave in lead II > 25 mm
- R wave in aVL > 11 mm
- Largest R wave plus largest S wave in precordial leads > 45 mm
After meeting voltage criteria, the EKG must also be accompanied by non-voltage
criteria:
- Increased R wave peak time > 50 ms in lead V5 or V6
- ST segment depression and T wave inversion in the left sided leads, called the
ventricular strain pattern
In the EKG shown here, we meet Sokolow-Lyon criteria, and we can see the presence of
the ventricular strain pattern.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
This patient should have an echocardiogram to assess the
LV function, quantify the degree of LV hypertrophy and assess whether hypertrophic
cardiomyopathy may be present. Additionally, better control of his hypertension is
warranted to prevent further remodeling of the LV.
cases.clinical_pearls:
LVH is most commonly caused by hypertension, due to the increased
afterload on the ventricle. Other causes include aortic valve stenosis, which also increases
afterload. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is another important cause of LVH, due to a
genetic mutation which causes significant wall thickening.

cases.case: A 59 year old male presents to primary care clinic for an annual physical. EKG is below.
basic_interpretation:
ST-T changes: ST depression / T wave inversion in lead I, aVL, V6; upsloping ST
elevation in V1-V3
Other findings: poor R wave progression
cases.explanation:
This EKG demonstrates a left bundle branch block (LBBB). There are a few
criteria here that go into diagnosing this. First, the QRS duration is prolonged > 120 ms.
Second, there is a predominant negative deflection Q/S wave in lead V1. Third, there are
broad, monophasic R waves in the lateral leads (I, aVL, V5-V6). LBBB often causes a
notching or M-shaped appearance to the QRS complex which we can best appreciate here
in leads III and V5. There are often associated findings of left ward axis deviation (not seen
here) and poor R wave progression (seen here).
Because the left bundle branch block results in abnormal myocardial depolarization
caused by the spread of impulses cell to cell from the RV to the LV instead of down the LBB
there is abnormal repolarization. In leads with deep S waves (usually V1-V4), there is often
ST segment elevation that does not necessarily indicate ischemia. The same is true for ST
segment depression in the lateral leads with tall R waves (usually I, aVL, V5-V6).
To diagnose ischemia in patients with a LBBB, we need to use the Sgarbossa criteria,
which generally suggest that ST segment elevation >25% of the preceding S wave is
pathologic and greater than 5mm (one big box).
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
If the patient is not having symptoms of chest pain
concerning for ischemia and is otherwise well, nothing necessarily needs to be done.
However, obtaining a non-urgent TTE would be reasonable to evaluate for underlying
cardiac dysfunction.
cases.clinical_pearls:
Patients who are ventricular paced (meaning they have a pacer lead in the
right ventricle) will have a left bundle branch morphology on EKG. The spread of the
impulse is very similar in both RV pacing and LBBB – electrical impulses travel into the right
ventricle and then must spread cell to cell to depolarize the left ventricle, resulting in a
wide QRS complex. Look out for pacer spikes on an EKG to distinguish these.

cases.case:
A 66-year-old female is evaluated in the emergency room for shortness of breath. EKG is
below.
basic_interpretation: ST-T changes: T wave inversions lead V1, biphasic T wave lead V2
cases.explanation:
This EKG demonstrates a right bundle branch block (RBBB). The criteria we
use to determine this are first, a QRS duration >120 ms. Second, there is an RSR’ pattern in
V1-V3. Third, the S wave in the lateral leads (I, aVL, V5-V6) is wide and has an almost
“slurred” appearance. Similar to left bundle branch blocks, when we have abnormal
depolarization, we can also expect abnormal repolarization. In the case of RBBB, this
means ST segment depression or T wave inversions in leads V1-V3.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
Nothing necessarily needs to be done immediately in the
case of a RBBB. A non-urgent echocardiogram can be performed to screen for structural
heart disease.
cases.clinical_pearls:
There are many potential causes of RBBB, including right ventricular
hypertrophy or cor pulmonale, ischemic heart disease, and cardiomyopathy. PE is also a
potential cause of RBBB and should be considered in this patient presenting with
shortness of breath.

cases.case: A 50 year old female is evaluated in the emergency room after an episode of syncope.
basic_interpretation: ST-T changes: diminished/flattened T waves throughout all leads
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows narrow QRS complexes without any consistent atrial activity.
This is called a junctional escape rhythm. We can identify this based on:
- Rate 40-60 bpm
- Narrow QRS complexes occurring at regular intervals (not irregularly irregular)
- No identifiable atrial activity (the baseline should be flat)
This rhythm occurs when the rate of the ectopic pacemaker (which is typically in the AV
node or in the His bundle for a junctional rhythm) is responsible for the rhythm typically
when the sinus node is not functioning as the pacemaker. The QRS complex is narrow
because the impulse is from the AV node still taking advantage of the His-Purkinje system.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
NBSIM depends on patient stability. If the patient has low
blood pressure or signs of end organ dysfunction, transcutaneous pacing and/or atropine is
warranted. If the patient is stable, they can be closely monitored on telemetry until
permanent pacemaker placement, unless a reversible cause is found. Check electrolights
and metabolic status. Discontinue AV node blocking drugs.
cases.clinical_pearls:
There are multiple causes that can lead to the emergence of a junctional
escape rhythm, which all relate to problems with conducting an impulse from the SA node
through the AV node. High 2nd degree and 3rd degree AV blocks, sinus node dysfunction,
severe sinus bradycardia, and medications such as beta blockers and calcium channel
blockers can all cause this.

cases.case:
A 72 year old female with a history of 2nd degree AV block presents to cardiology clinic for
routine follow up. EKG is below.
basic_interpretation: Rhythm: Sinus rhythm with demand ventricular pacing
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows ventricular pacing. This is evident from the pacer spikes
which precede each QRS complex. The rhythm is still sinus because P waves precede each
paced QRS complex. The patient must have a dual chamber (atrial and ventricular)
pacemaker such that there is A-sensed V-pacing, where the lead in the atrium is sensing
the atrial depolarization, and then communicating to the lead in the ventricle to initiate
pacing of the ventricles. Ventricular pacing is typically from the RV apex that produces a
LBBB-like pattern on EKG, because the impulse from the RV spreads cell to cell and slowly
depolarizes the RV. The T wave inversions in multiple leads represent abnormal
repolarization from abnormal depolarization.
cases.clinical_pearls:
Over time, patients with right ventricular pacing can develop a pacer-
induced cardiomyopathy. The etiology of this is thought to be due to abnormal synchrony
of depolarization resulting in asynchronous contraction of the ventricles. Newer
pacemakers use other strategies such as biventricular pacing or conduction system
pacing to try to more closely simulate the natural conduction system.

cases.case:
A 39-year-old male presents to primary care clinic with complaint of frequent palpitations.
EKG is below.
basic_interpretation:
Rhythm: sinus with frequent premature ventricular contractions (PVCs, identified by
the wide QRS complexes occurring in a “trigeminy” pattern with two sinus beats
followed by one PVC.
QRS interval: normal in sinus beats, wide in PVCs
ST-T changes: ST depressions/T wave inversions following PVCs
Interpretation: sinus rhythm with PVCs / ventricular trigeminy
cases.explanation:
Premature ventricular complexes arise from ectopic pacemaker cells in the
ventricles. Because the impulse arises from the ventricles and is not propagated down the
His-Purkinje system, the morphology of the QRS complex is wide (QRS > 120 ms). The
impulse comes prematurely (earlier than the next sinus beat would occur). Since there is
abnormal depolarization, we also expect abnormal repolarization (appropriate
discordance), for example ST segment depression and T wave inversions following the PVCs.
PVCs may occur in isolation or in patterns, as we see above. This pattern, where there is a
PVC on every third beat, is called trigeminy. If there is a PVC every other beat, this would be
called bigeminy.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
PVCs are frequently benign but can be associated with
underlying heart disease. In this patient with frequent PVCs, it would be good to obtain an
echo to assess cardiac function. If the PVCs are very frequent and symptomatic, first line
treatment is with a beta blocker or calcium channel blocker.
cases.clinical_pearls:
Frequent PVCs can cause a PVC-induced cardiomyopathy. This decrease
in cardiac function is typically reversible with medical therapy or catheter PVC ablation.

cases.case:
A 56-year-old female is admitted to the hospital for an elective knee replacement. Pre-op
EKG is below.
cases.explanation:
The PAC in this EKG may not jump out immediately. If you look closely at the
rhythm strip, you can see there is slight irregularity. Zooming in on this complex, we notice
it is not preceded by a normal P wave that is shown in lead II. However, the QRS is the
same morphology as the other QRS complexes consistent with the premature impulse
coming from the atrium. Following this complex, there is a pause before the next sinus P
wave. This complex is a premature atrial complex (PAC). This complex originates from an
ectopic pacemaker in the atria that sends signals down the His-Purkinje system, producing
a normal QRS complex. The P wave often looks abnormal in a PAC because the atrial
depolarization is abnormal.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
PACs are a normal electrical phenomenon and typically do
not require further workup or treatment unless they are frequent and symptomatic.
cases.clinical_pearl:
In patients with an underlying predisposition, a PAC may trigger an arrythmia
such as atrial fibrillation, flutter, AVNRT or AVRT.

cases.case:
A 58-year-old male with a history of diabetes presents to the emergency room with
subacute dyspnea and lower extremity edema. EKG is below.
basic_interpretation: ST-T changes: T wave inversions in the anterior leads (V1-V3)
Other findings: “Pathologic” Q waves in II, III, aVF
Interpretation: Q waves and T wave inversions consistent with prior MI
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows sinus rhythm with ischemic changes consistent with a prior
myocardial infarction, with the development of Q waves (negative deflections that precede
the R wave) and T wave inversions. Small Q waves can be normal, but Q waves that are > 2
mm or >25% of the total QRS amplitude are considered pathologic.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
This patient should be evaluated for active ischemia with
troponin levels as well as evaluation of heart failure with clinical exam, BNP, and TTE.
cases.clinical_pearl:
Patients with diabetes are more likely to have “silent” MIs, meaning they do
not experience clinical symptoms that bring them into the hospital at the time of the event.

cases.case: A 70-year-old female with a history of COPD presents with palpitations. EKG is below.
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows a “sawtooth” baseline of P waves consistent with atrial
flutter, but a normal ventricular rate. There are 4 P waves prior to each QRS complex, so we
describe this as atrial flutter with a 4:1 block. Recall that the atrial flutter rate is
approximately 280-300 bpm. The AV node does not conduct all of these impulses to the
ventricles, and in this case only every 4th atrial depolarization is conducted to the AV node.
This is a bit atypical, and flutter is more likely to be conducted 2:1. This relatively slow
ventricular rate suggests treatment with an AV nodal blocking agent (such as a beta
blocker or adenosine), or an underlying AV block.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
Check for stability! If the patient’s blood pressure is low,
they should undergo emergent cardioversion. With a slow ventricular rate such as in this
patient, they are unlikely to have hemodynamic compromise from this rhythm. If the
patient is stable, they can be started on anticoagulation based on CHADS2VASC and initial
management can focus on rate control with beta blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium
channel blockers.
cases.clinical_pearl:
Remember that the flutter rate is 280-300 bpm and it is rare for that rate to
be conducted to the ventricles. If you see a rate of 140-150 which is regular and stays very
consistent, you should think about atrial flutter.

cases.case: A 30-year-old healthy male has a screening EKG at a health fair. The EKG is below.
basic_interpretation: ST-T changes: diffuse T wave inversions, ST depression in the precordial leads
Other findings: delta wave, Q wave lead III
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows the upsloping/slurred delta wave of the QRS complex, which
is seen in WPW. This indicates the presence of an accessory pathway that conducts
impulses from the atria to the ventricles. This accessory pathway conducts slightly faster
than the AV node initially, which is why we get early depolarization and that upsloping delta
wave, as well as a relatively short PR interval. The AV node and His-Purkinje system then
catches up and takes over, so the resulting QRS remains narrow. Because there is
abnormal depolarization, we also see abnormal repolarization, in this case ST
depressions, T wave inversions, and a Q wave in lead III that mimic ischemic changes.
Additionally, we see high amplitude QRS complexes along with the T wave inversions in the
precordial leads that mimics LVH, but are actually due to the WPW pathway.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
Seeing a WPW pattern on EKG in an asymptomatic patient
does not require any urgent workup. However, the WPW pattern predisposes to atrial
arrhythmias and poses a risk of sudden cardiac death (although this is quite rare). This
patient should be referred to a cardiologist and be advised to report any symptoms of
palpitations to a physician. Heart rhythm monitoring for 2-weeks can also be used to
screen for SVT.
cases.clinical_pearl:
The arrythmia that is most associated with WPW is AVRT. This occurs due to
retrograde conduction from the ventricles to the atria through the accessory pathway,
creating a circuit involving the AV node and the accessory pathway.

cases.case:
A 34-year-old female admitted to the hospital for gastroenteritis has a sudden increase in
heart rate on telemetry and complains of palpitations. EKG is below.
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows a regular, narrow complex tachycardia. This narrows the
differential to a supraventricular tachycardia, likely AVNRT or AVRT. These arrhythmias are
typically precipitated by a PAC or PVC, and result in an abnormal circuit of electrical
activation – involving either the slow and fast pathways of the AV node in AVNRT, or the AV
node and the accessory pathway in AVRT. In this scenario, the management of both will be
the same, but if you want to get into the specifics, there are clues in the EKG to further
narrow down the diagnosis. Both of these rhythms cause abnormal depolarization of the
atria (impulses travel retrograde up through the AV node instead of the SA node), so there
is a retrograde (inverted) P wave. In AVNRT, the retrograde P wave is usually not visible, as
it is buried in the QRS complex. AVRT has a longer R-P interval, and you can see a
retrograde P wave in this EKG as a notch in the T wave.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
If the patient’s blood pressure is low, they should have
urgent electrical cardioversion. If stable, you can first try vagal maneuvers (ie having the
patient bear down or blow into a straw/syringe), and if these are unsuccessful you should
administer adenosine.
cases.clinical_pearl:
AVRT occurs when there is an accessory pathway as well as appropriate
stimulus, most commonly in WPW. You should obtain an EKG after the episode of
tachycardia is resolved to assess for a delta wave.

cases.case:
A 56-year-old male undergoes a CABG operation. Several hours after the procedure, he
complains of dizziness and an EKG is performed.
basic_interpretation: PR interval: abnormal, no P waves
ST segment – T wave changes: T wave inversions in the anterior/lateral leads
Interpretation: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows an irregularly irregular rhythm, with no discernible P waves
consistent with atrial fibrillation. In addition, the ventricular rate is fast (150). Typically
rates in afib >100 can be considered “rapid ventricular response”. This EKG also
demonstrates some T wave changes which may represent some demand ischemia and
abnormal repolarization in the setting of very fast heart rates.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
Assess for stability! If the BP is low (this patient is reporting
dizziness, which could be due to tachycardia alone or to resultant hypotension), this
patient will require urgent cardioversion. If their blood pressure is stable, initial rate control
with beta blockers is appropriate.
cases.clinical_pearl:
Atrial fibrillation is common after cardiac surgery and occurs in 15-40% of
patients after CABG. These patients commonly have spontaneous conversion to sinus
rhythm.

cases.case:
A 67-year-old female presents to the emergency room with lightheadedness and
presyncope. EKG is below.
basic_interpretation: Rhythm: sinus with 2nd degree AV block
PR interval: normal w/dropped beat, 2nd degree AV block Mobitz II
ST segment – T wave changes: none
Other findings: poor R wave progression, left anterior fascicular block
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows bradycardia with a normal PR interval but a non-conducted
“dropped” beat (P wave without a following QRS complex). The PR intervals are consistent
without progressive lengthening, so this is consistent with a 2nd degree AV block, Mobitz 2.
There is additionally a left anterior fascicular block with a resultant left axis deviation. The
left bundle branch divides further into two main branches, the left anterior fascicle and the
left posterior fascicle. When there is a block along the left anterior fascicle, we get a
distinct EKG pattern of qR complexes in lead I and aVL, and rS complexes in lead II, III, and
aVF, as well as a left axis deviation.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
This patient should be admitted for telemetry monitoring
and pacemaker implant. Any potentially reversible causes of AV block, such as
medications, Lyme disease, thyroid disease should be sought.
cases.clinical_pearl:
Left anterior fascicular block has many possible etiologies, but it is
important to consider hypertension, coronary artery disease, aortic valve disease,
cardiomyopathy, and obstructive sleep apnea.

cases.case:
A 61-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital last month for an MI presents to the
ED with chest pain.
basic_interpretation: PR interval: normal (but very difficult to see p waves here)
ST-T changes: diffuse ST elevations – II, III, aVF and precordial leads
cases.explanation:
This EKG demonstrates widespread ST elevations throughout most of the limb
leads and precordial leads, consistent with pericarditis. Other findings that you can expect
to see in pericarditis are ST depressions in lead aVR +/- lead V1, and PR segment
depression (which is not super evident in this EKG). The ST elevations are usually minimal
and concave. Sinus tachycardia is also a common finding, and can be secondary to pain or
to a pericardial effusion.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
This patient should be admitted for further workup. He
should have troponin levels to rule out active ischemia, and undergo a TTE to assess for a
pericardial effusion.
cases.clinical_pearl:
Dressler’s syndrome is an immune-mediated pericarditis which occurs in
the weeks following myocardial infarction.

cases.case:
An 82-year-old female with a history of COPD is admitted to the hospital for fevers and
shortness of breath. EKG is below.
basic_interpretation: ST-T changes: diffuse T wave flattening
Other findings: poor R wave progression
Interpretation: sinus with R axis deviation
cases.explanation:
This EKG demonstrates right axis deviation – there is a primarily downwardly
deflected QRS complex in lead I, and primarily upwardly deflected QRS complexes in lead
II and aVF. Additionally, there is poor R wave progression, which is seen in the precordial
leads. There should be a transition from a dominant S wave to a dominant R wave around
lead V3-V4. Here that transition does not occur until V5.
cases.clinical_pearl:
There are several important possible causes of right axis deviation, including
RVH and lateral MI. There are no other findings to suggest these causes in this EKG. Right
axis deviation is also associated with acute and chronic pulmonary disease, due to stress
on the right side of the heart. Right axis deviation in general is a reliable indicator of
underlying structural heart disease whereas left axis deviation can be found in up to 5% of
people with no cardiac disease.

cases.case: A 30-year-old female presents to the emergency room with palpitations. EKG is below.
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows a regular, narrow complex tachycardia. The P waves are not
visible before the QRS complexes, so it is not sinus rhythm. This is an SVT. The differential
for regular, narrow complex tachycardia is AVRT, AVNRT, and focal atrial tachycardia.
Focal atrial tachycardia is rare. AVNRT is the most common, and AVRT exists in the
presence of an accessory pathway. AVNRT and AVRT can also be distinguished by the R-P
interval. The retrograde P waves are visible here at the end of the QRS complex. This is a
short R-P interval consistent with AVNRT.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
Assess the patient’s blood pressure. If low, cardiovert! If
stable, you can trial vagal maneuvers (bear down, have the patient blow into a straw or
syringe), and if unsuccessful administer adenosine.

cases.case:
A 64-year-old male with a history of coronary artery disease presents after an episode of
syncope.
basic_interpretation: Rhythm: sinus rhythm with complete heart block and a junctional escape rhythm
ST-T changes: ST depressions in V5-V6, large T waves in the precordial leads
Interpretation: complete heart block with a junctional escape rhythm
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows independent P waves and QRS complexes, consistent with 3rd
degree AV block or complete heart block. The ventricular rhythm is a junctional escape
rhythm (rate 40-60, narrow QRS, and no relationship to the atrial activity). There is
additionally evidence of LVH by Sokolow-Lyon criteria (S wave in V1+largest R wave in
V5/V6 >35 mm). The ST depressions and large T waves in the precordial leads are likely due
to LV strain.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
This patient should be admitted to the hospital for telemetry
monitoring. If blood pressure is stable, he can be monitored until permanent pacer implantation,
but if it is unstable or the ventricular rate drops, he should receive atropine and have a temporary pacing lead inserted.
cases.clinical_pearl:
Important causes of complete heart block include advanced age, ischemia
(inferior MI), premature degenerative disease of the conduction system (Lenegre’s
disease), and AV nodal blocking drugs.

cases.case:
A 72-year-old male presents to the emergency room with chest pain and dizziness. EKG is
below.
basic_interpretation: Rhythm: wide complex tachycardia: ventricular tachycardia
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows a wide QRS complex tachycardia. There is a limited
differential for this, and the most important cause is ventricular tachycardia. This rhythm is
regular, with broad, uniform QRS complexes. VT can be monomorphic, as we see here,
where the QRS complexes are similar in appearance. It can also be polymorphic, with
multiple morphologies to the QRS complexes. The differential diagnosis is SVT with
aberrancy.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
You should follow ACLS guidelines here. If there is
hypotension, altered mental status, signs of shock, acute heart failure, or ischemic chest
discomfort (as we see in this patient), you should perform synchronized cardioversion. If
the patient is stable, you can start antiarrhythmic infusions (amiodarone) and consult a
cardiologist.
cases.clinical_pearl:
Ischemic heart disease is an important cause of ventricular arrhythmias.
After cardioversion, this patient should have a repeat EKG to evaluate for ischemia.

cases.case: A 39-year-old female presents to urgent care for a rash and palpitations. EKG is below.
basic_interpretation: Rhythm: Sinus rhythm with 2:1 AV block
PR interval: prolonged and dropped beats
ST-T changes: T wave inversions in V1-V3
Interpretation: 2nd degree AV block, 2:1 with RBBB
cases.explanation:
This EKG shows a slightly prolonged PR interval, followed by a P wave with a
dropped QRS complex. Every other P wave is not followed by a QRS complex. Since we
have dropped beats, we are dealing with a 2nd degree AV block. However, we cannot
definitively classify this as Mobitz I or Mobitz II. Since every other beat is dropped, we can’t
tell whether the PR interval is prolonging. This is called a 2:1 AV block or fixed ratio block.
We additionally see a right bundle branch block in this EKG – the QRS is >120 ms (3 small
boxes) and there is an RSR’ in leads V1-V3 as well as appropriate discordance (abnormal
repolarization following abnormal depolarization) with inverted T waves.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
This patient should be admitted to the hospital for telemetry
monitoring. If blood pressure is unstable, she should receive atropine and transcutaneous
pacing followed by a temporary pacing wire. If she is stable, reversible causes of heart block
should be evaluated and there are none, should have a permanent pacer placed.
cases.clinical_pearl:
Lyme disease is an important cause of AV block to consider in patients with
possible exposures or symptoms of fevers or rash.

cases.case:
A 70-year-old female with a history of hypertension is admitted for cholecystitis. EKG is
below.
basic_interpretation: ST-T changes: ST elevation in V2-V3, T wave inversions in the inferior leads
Other findings: poor R wave progression
cases.explanation:
This EKG demonstrates a few abnormalities. First, there is a left bundle
branch block – the QRS interval is prolonged >120 ms (3 small boxes) and there is a broad
negative R wave in leads V4-V6. There is appropriate discordance (abnormal repolarization
after abnormal depolarization) seen in the ST elevations in V2 and V3, as well as the T wave
inversions in the inferior leads (II, III, aVF). There is additionally a left axis deviation, seen by
the negative QRS complex in lead I, and positive QRS in leads II and aVF. There is also poor R
wave progression in the precordial leads, with an R wave height < 3 mm in lead V3. We can see
that the lateral leads (V4-V6) do not switch to being upright with a predominant R wave,
which we would expect in a normal EKG. Both the LAD and poor R wave progression are
associated with left bundle branch blocks.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
If the patient is not having symptoms of chest pain concerning for ischemia and is
otherwise well, there is likely no immediate next step. However, obtaining a non-urgent TTE
would be reasonable to evaluate for underlying cardiac dysfunction.
cases.clinical_pearls:
LBBB often reflects underlying structural cardiac disease, such as hypertension, coronary
artery disease, or cardiomyopathies which lead to fibrosis and destruction of the conduction
system.

cases.case:
A 65-year-old female with a history of breast cancer presents with shortness of breath.
EKG is below.
basic_interpretation: ST-T changes: T wave inversions V1-V4
cases.explanation:
This EKG demonstrates a right bundle branch block. There is a wide QRS complex (>120
ms), an RSR’ complex in leads V1-V3, and a wide, slurred S wave in the lateral leads (I, aVL,
V5-V6). There is abnormal depolarization so there is also abnormal repolarization
(appropriate discordance), seen in the T wave inversions in the precordial leads (V1-V4).
cases.next_best_step_in_management_clinical_pe:
While a right bundle branch is nonspecific, in this patient with risk factors for
hypercoagulability, sinus tachycardia, and shortness of breath, it may indicate a
pulmonary embolism. She should have imaging (CTA chest) to look for this.

cases.case:
A 25-year-old male is evaluated in the cardiology clinic after an episode of syncope. EKG is
below.
basic_interpretation:
ST-T changes: ST depressions and T wave inversions in the lateral leads (I, aVL, V3-
V6)
cases.explanation:
This EKG demonstrates LVH. Recall there are numerous voltage criteria for diagnosing
LVH. The most common is Sokolow-Lyon, with height of the S wave in V1 + the height of the
tallest R wave in V5 or V6 >35 mm, which this EKG meets. There is also the presence of ST
depressions and T wave inversions in leads I, aVL, V3-V6, which is known as the left
ventricular strain pattern. This is a non-voltage criteria for LVH.
cases.next_best_step_in_management:
This young patient with syncope and evidence of LVH should be evaluated for hypertrophic
cardiomyopathy with an echocardiogram.

cases.case:
An 82-year-old female with a history of sinus node disease presents for follow-up. EKG is
below.
cases.explanation:
This EKG is a little tricky, because the atrial pacing spikes are very small and don’t initially
stand out. However, you can see that there is a small pacer spike preceding each of the P
waves. There are no pacer spikes preceding the QRS complexes. This could be
representative of a single chamber (atrial) pacemaker, or a dual chamber (AV pacing) with
an underlying/native rhythm that is inhibiting the ventricular pacing.
cases.clinical_pearl:
There are few indications for atrial pacing alone. These include symptomatic sinus
bradycardia, sick sinus syndrome, and tachy-brady syndrome. This would not work for any
degree of AV block, as the atrial impulse may not be conducted through the AV block to the
ventricles.
